items.title: OpenAI ships gpt-realtime-2.1 and 2.1-mini: 25% lower latency, reasoning while talking
items.summary: OpenAI released two new Realtime voice models (July 6–7, 2026). Both cut p95 latency ~25% via better caching. The headline is gpt-realtime-2.1-mini: a distilled reasoning model that keeps talking while it runs a tool call — no dead silence — at the same price as the old mini ($10 audio-in / $20 audio-out per 1M vs $32 / $64 for the full model).
items.whyItMatters: Romain runs on OpenAI Realtime — dropping to 2.1-mini could roughly halve Romain's voice cost while removing the awkward pause when he calls save_vocab or web_search mid-conversation.

items.title: DeepSeek V4 official launch — 75% price cut is now permanent (Flash at $0.14/$0.28)
items.summary: DeepSeek's V4 family moves from April preview to an official mid-July 2026 release. The steep launch discount is now the permanent list price: V4 Pro at $0.435 in / $0.87 out and V4 Flash at $0.14 / $0.28 per 1M tokens, both with a 1M-token context and MIT license. Note the new 2× peak-hour pricing during Beijing business windows, and that legacy deepseek-chat/reasoner aliases stop working July 24.
items.whyItMatters: The tutor already lists DeepSeek as an invokeLLM fallback — V4 Flash is cheap enough to make it the primary for dictionary lookups and grammar generation, cutting per-request LLM cost dramatically.

items.title: Cartesia launches Sonic-3.5: sub-90ms TTS, 42 languages including French
items.summary: Cartesia introduced Sonic-3.5 (with Ink-2 STT), pitched as one provider for your whole voice stack — ranked #1 for naturalness with ~90ms model latency and native support for 42 languages. It's built on State Space Models rather than transformers, giving strong latency for real-time voice agents.
items.whyItMatters: A concrete lower-latency, lower-cost TTS alternative to ElevenLabs for Anna — worth A/B testing French voice quality against Flash v2.5, especially given Anna's WebSocket streaming setup.

items.title: Gemini 2.5 Flash Native Audio upgrade beats gpt-realtime on function-calling
items.summary: Google's updated Gemini 2.5 Flash Native Audio model improves conversational sound, raises developer-instruction adherence to 90% (from 84%), and scores 71.5% on the ComplexFuncBench Audio benchmark — ahead of OpenAI's gpt-realtime. Google also shipped streaming speech-to-speech translation across 70+ languages, with newer gemini-3.x-flash-live audio models now in preview.
items.whyItMatters: A third viable real-time voice backend to benchmark for a French tutor — strong function-calling matters because both Romain and Anna rely on tool calls (save_vocab), and the live-translation model hints at a 'translate on demand' feature.

items.title: Tavus CVI: one API for a real-time talking video avatar (~600ms, Phoenix-4)
items.summary: Tavus's Conversational Video Interface replaces a 5–6 vendor stack (WebRTC + STT + LLM + TTS + lip-sync + orchestration) with a single API for face-to-face AI video. Its Phoenix-4 model renders every frame live at 40fps with 10+ emotional states, at ~600ms end-to-end latency over 30+ languages, with React/iframe/LiveKit integration.
items.whyItMatters: This is the productized path to give Romain (and Anna) a genuinely animated, reactive video avatar instead of the current looping clip — directly on-theme with the split-screen tutor UI you just shipped.

items.title: Duolingo makes AI Video Call with Lily free for everyone (French supported)
items.summary: As of January 2026 Duolingo's Video Call — live spoken conversation with the AI character Lily, with on-the-fly grammar correction, memory of past calls, expressive animations, transcripts, and even 'Lily calls you' nudges — is free to all users and available for French learners. Their engineering blog details how they constrain the model with targeted instructions and a predictable call structure.
items.whyItMatters: This is the closest competitor to your voice tutor, now free — the memory system, post-call transcripts, and structured-prompt approach are all directly worth copying for Romain/Anna.

items.title: Speak (OpenAI-backed, $1B) doubles down on 'speak out loud from day one', adds French
items.summary: Speak, the AI speaking-first tutor backed by the OpenAI Startup Fund, is valued at $1B and expanding into Europe with new languages including French. Its philosophy — get you talking from lesson one via AI speaking drills and an open 'Speak Tutor' conversation mode rather than tapping flashcards — earned it a spot in Wirecutter's 2026 best-apps list; users have now spoken 1B+ sentences.
items.whyItMatters: A validated blueprint for the speaking-first thesis behind your voice chat — their mix of short scripted drills plus open conversation is a useful model for structuring Romain/Anna sessions beyond free chat.
